title: Algorithmic and Minimax Complexities in Kernel Bandits
abstract: Gaussian-process upper confidence bound (GP-UCB) and decision-estimation-coefficient (DEC) methods may appear, at first sight, to belong to different theories. This paper places the two viewpoints in a common algorithmic-information language for frequentist RKHS bandits. GP-UCB fixes an algorithmic, rather than true, Gaussian-process prior and exploits realized-trajectory complexity together with computational tractability, whereas MAMS optimizes a robust class-wide MAIR/DEC envelope. Through the unified MAIR framework and heterogeneous positive-semidefinite algorithmic priors, we generalize both the GP-UCB analysis and the MAMS algorithm, propose a safeguarded master that combines their advantages, and provide a kernel-bandit construction showing that algorithmic complexity can be more informative than class-wide minimax or DEC certificates in overparameterized models. The resulting message is that algorithmic information and class-wide minimax coefficients answer different questions and can lead to different gaps; kernel bandits provide a clean setting in which this distinction becomes mathematically visible.
summary: This paper develops a unified framework to compare performance guarantees for sequential learning algorithms like GP-UCB and MAMS in kernel bandit problems. It mathematically proves that the information gained by an algorithm based on its specific prior (algorithmic view) can differ significantly from the worst-case guarantee derived over the entire model class (minimax view). This distinction is crucial for selecting appropriate theoretical bounds.
main_problem: The paper aims to unify and compare three distinct theoretical views of sequential learning performance—algorithmic, information-theoretic/minimax, and class-wide worst-case regret—within the context of kernel bandits.
main_result: It demonstrates that algorithmic information gain (from GP-UCB) and class-wide minimax coefficients answer different questions, providing a clean setting in kernel bandits where this distinction is mathematically visible.
method: The analysis uses unified MAIR frameworks, deriving identities linking Bayesian posterior updates to realized algorithmic information gain, and comparing these against saddle-point optimization criteria for minimax bounds.
model_or_system: The model is a frequentist linear/kernel truth $f^\star(x)$ in an RKHS, observed with Gaussian noise. The setting is generalized using heterogeneous positive-semidefinite (PSD) algorithmic priors ($\Gamma$) and involves comparing performance across structured truths versus worst-case class bounds.
key_observables: Regret ($R_T$), Algorithmic Information Gain ($I_{T,\Gamma}$), Minimax Regret-Information Coefficient, and the Model-index Algorithmic Information Ratio (MAIR).
important_parameters: The complexity is governed by the structure of the kernel class ($\Gamma$) and the separation gap between hub and cloud components in the model space.
main_assumptions: Assumes conditional R-sub-Gaussian noise for the observations. The analysis relies on establishing specific identities (like the Posterior-reference density-ratio telescope) that link different forms of information accumulation.
figures_summary: Not specified, but the notes indicate comparisons between regret bounds derived from UCB, MAMS, and minimax lower bounds in various constructions (e.g., hub-cloud).
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing foundational identities linking Bayesian updates to information gain (Theorem 2.1), then unifying this structure via the MAIR objective (Lemma 3.2), applying these concepts to show GP-UCB satisfies an MAIR condition, and finally comparing against finite class bounds using MAMS.
why_interesting: While not directly about quantum systems, the rigorous comparison of different complexity measures (algorithmic vs. worst-case) mirrors theoretical challenges in characterizing resource limitations in complex physical models where multiple optimization criteria might apply simultaneously.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
